data.title: BDC Weekly Review: CGBD Completes Its Merger
data.description:
Welcome to another installment of our BDC Market Weekly Review, where we discuss market activity in the Business Development Company [BDC] sector from both the bottom-up - highlighting individual news and events - as well as the top-down - providing an overview of the broader market.

We also try to add some historical context as well as relevant themes that look to be driving the market or that investors ought to be mindful of. This update covers the period through the last week of March.

Market Action

BDCs finished flat on the week with only modest volatility, in contrast to the preceding few weeks. Through March, only a couple of stocks in our coverage are up, however. Both MAIN and HTGC - the highest valuation BDCs have underperformed in the recent downdraft - a common pattern.

The average valuation in our coverage remains slightly above its historic average. It has come off around 7% from the recent peak.

Market Themes

Carlyle Secured Lending (CGBD) had its merger proposal with a private affiliated BDC CSL III approved. The transaction closed last week. Recall that CGBD got downgraded by BOA on the merger news. The view is that the merger will release previously private shareholders who will now be able to sell CGBD shares in the market, pushing the price lower.

CGBD is one of the few BDCs that is down on the year (-3.5% vs. a +2.5% average gain). The stock is slightly expensive still, but it’s worth watching in case it drops into attractive territory. Its 10.5% total NAV return over the past year is a bit below the median of 11.5% (though above the average) while its valuation is on par with the median (and below the average).

The valuation relative to the sector has come off around 8% from its peak.

Market Commentary

A reader asked about the view on the Palmer Square Capital BDC (PSBD). The discount looks attractive at around 13%. However, there are some issues as well. For one, leverage is one of the highest in the sector (perhaps second highest after Saratoga) at 1.5x vs. an average of closer to 1.1x. This is something that creates a large dose of downside risk. It also makes net investment income relatively inefficient.

Net investment income is only 42% of total investment income which is fairly low in the sector. This is also due to the very high management fee of 1.75% as well as the cost of debt which is slightly above average.

The company’s NAV has been steady recently, if on a slight downtrend, however there were earlier drops, particularly in 2022, which have not been clawed back. The company’s NAV was over $20 in 2021 and it is $16.50 now. The total NAV return over the past year looks to be around 6% - well below the sector average.

The key year looks to be 2022 when the stock lost about a quarter of its NAV.

If we break it down, we see that the majority of that was due to unrealized depreciation. However, what's more interesting is that only half of the unrealized depreciation was reversed over 2023.

More recently, the performance has been steadier if not on par with the overall sector. We would look for more stability in the portfolio before adding a position.

data.title: Otis Worldwide: Cautiously Optimistic Approaching Earnings (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Introduction

In my original analysis of Otis Worldwide (NYSE:OTIS), I assigned the stock a Hold rating, citing its premium valuation and weak momentum. At the time, I noted the strength of its business model—centered around stable, high-margin recurring revenue from elevator and escalator servicing—but questioned whether these attributes could justify its valuation given potential headwinds. Since that assessment, however, the stock has notably outperformed the broader market. This phenomenon gained my attention notably on April 3rd, 2025, the day of this writing, when the stock was modestly up by 25 bps while the S&P 500 sold off almost 5% on tariff jitters. While not much has changed about the underlying business performance outlook since my last article, it seems that the market is beginning to understand and appreciate the defensive qualities of this stock much more, giving me good reason to revisit an analysis. In this article, I will discuss the reasons why I believe a rating upgrade from Hold to Buy is now appropriate for OTIS.

Company Overview

OTIS is, of course, a global player in manufacturing, installing, and servicing elevators and escalators, with a leading market share of roughly 18%. The company's recession-resistant economics hinge primarily on its highly profitable service business, which accounts for around two-thirds of total revenue but approximately 85% of operating profit. Its recurring maintenance contracts are typically long-term and stable, driven by strict safety regulations and the essential nature of its products. Better yet, they are asset-light and produce very strong recession resistant cashflows. The company does the majority of its business overseas, which was highlighted as a risk in the prior article but will be explored with more depth today.

Since becoming a standalone entity after spinning off from United Technologies in the depths of the 2020 COVID crash, Otis has made significant efforts to bolster shareholder returns through dividends and share buybacks. Its dividend growth has been particularly impressive, doubling over the past four years, representing roughly 17% annual growth. While I do not expect dividend growth to remain so elevated over the long term, it has driven significant shareholder returns and brought attention to the stock from dividend-focused investors. Despite the underlying business having very defensive qualities, the stock has not traded defensively. However, over the last few months, the stock has appeared to diverge into a new era of performance which I will demonstrate in this writing.

Revisiting the Original Thesis

My previous analysis identified key risks, notably Otis's significant exposure to China and currency volatility due to its international revenue streams. Indeed, China accounts for a substantial portion of global new elevator demand and management noted in the most recent earnings report that 13% of 2024 sales were in CNY. Recent years have shown a steady decline in this market, with the company itself acknowledging multiple consecutive years of weakness. However, recent shifts in the currency market, especially the weakening of the U.S. dollar in response to new tariff policies, might create near-term tailwinds for Otis’s earnings given that a considerable percentage of its revenues come from Europe (23% of sales in Euros) and Asia. Management is guiding toward 4-7% EPS growth in FY 2025 but put that FX would be a headwind on earnings. With the DXY sliding this year so far and USD only representing about 30% of sales, earnings could receive a nice tailwind from FX over the next two earnings reports. It is unclear whether or not this dollar weakness will persist for a meaningful time or if it is just temporary, but one thing that was true about Trump 1.0 was that the dollar was weaker when Trump left office the first time vs. his inauguration, portending his willingness to wield a weaker dollar to accomplish trade policy aims.

Otis's share price performance relative to the broader market indicates a newfound defensive trading posture that it never seemed to demonstrate before. The company has now exceeded the returns of the S&P 500 by roughly 20% YTD. While this is not exactly surprising, it is unexpected since the stock did not really have a history of low correlation to the index. While it is unclear if this trend will continue, it is a positive sign for the company's prospects as a portfolio holding in the upcoming years of trade turbulence. Not only do the revenues of the company straddle multiple currencies and continents, but they are recession resistant and "essential", perfect for outperformance in a turbulent macroeconomic environment.

Valuation and Financial Performance

Since my last writing, nothing has materially changed regarding the outlook of the underlying business performance. It is still richly valued, especially when considering a mid single digit % EPS growth rate.

Currently, Otis trades at a PE multiple of approximately 25, reflecting continued investor confidence in the stability of its service-based cash flows, despite relatively modest revenue growth in recent quarters. Otis has maintained robust free cash flow generation, recently averaging around a 4% free cash flow yield. While not cheap, this valuation is beginning to appear more reasonable given the shifting macroeconomic landscape and relative stability of its earnings power. There are a few defensive industrial services businesses that come to mind, such as Republic Services (RSG) and Waste Management (WM) that have performed exceptionally well in recent months and are now much more richly valued than OTIS. Interestingly, OTIS and these stocks appear to be highly correlated with each other as of late, which is a bit of a departure from the way things were before.

One thing I highlighted last time was that shareholders were benefitting from overly aggressive capital return, whereby the company was increasing dividends at a higher rate than the growth of the underlying cash flows and that this implied there are not many avenues for value accretion through M&A. Interestingly, management guided that they would actually be buying back fewer shares in 2025 than they did in 2024, despite expecting roughly the same level of free cash flow, guiding to $800 million of share repurchases in 2025 vs. $1 billion in 2024. They estimate that this will accrete roughly 5 cents to adjusted EPS for the year.

All in all, the company recorded a fairly strong year for 2024 with adjusted EPS growing by 8.2%, and Service Portfolio units increasing by 4.2%. With Service as the primary profit driver and the company wielding very strong Operating Leverage, Service Portfolio unit growth in excess of inflation alone should translate to meaningful share price performance over time. Management did guide for Global installed base unit growth to be in the mid-single digits for 2025, implying another year of slow and steady compounding for Service Portfolio units. The market loves companies that are slow and steady with wide moat characteristics, and the trading behavior we saw as the broad market slid steeply is a clue that investors are warming up to OTIS as a defensive play.

Dividend Growth and Sustainability

It would be accurate to label OTIS historically as a dividend growth stock, as the dividend per share has basically doubled since the spin-off. However, I think it is more appropriate for prospective investors to think of the stock in the go-forward as a defensive industrial that happens to pay a growing dividend. While the historic 15-17% annual dividend growth rate should moderate to the high single digits over the long term, this remains attractive given current market conditions. Given its resilient cash flow and stable margins, Otis should comfortably sustain meaningful dividend increases, supported by ongoing share repurchases enhancing earnings per share. One thing I am very anxiously awaiting in this upcoming earnings season for OTIS is how much the dividend raise will be. I would prefer to see a raise exactly in line with their EPS guidance for the year on the high end of 7-8%, but we will see. While I would benefit personally from another mid-teens % raise, I would see it more positively if management were to increase the payout in line with the sustainable growth rate. That being said, I suppose management has some runway to increase dividends at a double-digit rate since the current payout ratio is modest at roughly 40%.

Technical Analysis

From a technical perspective, not much has changed, with the stock now approaching the all-time high once again. My previous article was written just after the price had failed to gain steam after briefly reaching a new all-time high and falling back somewhat. Now, with the macroeconomic backdrop looking modestly favorable for strength in the stock and earnings approaching, it is time to test a breakout again. With the USD weakening in the first quarter, there is a likelihood that OTIS could beat earnings on FX alone. While it isn't straightforward if this would lead to a breakout if it did happen, I am cautiously optimistic that we could be seeing a modest breakout in the near future. For potential buyers, watching closely for another strong volume breakout above recent highs or a confirmed bounce from lower trendline support could offer a compelling entry point, although I see the current level as being fine to enter as well. I would reassess if the stock once again failed to break out meaningfully.

Final Assessment: Upgrading to Buy on Greater Momentum

After re-examining my initial Hold rating on Otis, I now view the stock in a slightly more positive light due to shifting macroeconomic factors and recent trading behaviors. I specifically mentioned "weak momentum" as a defining characteristic in the title of my last piece, but namely I believe that this is either a non factor now or is soon to turn over. The company's strong service-driven economic model and resilient financial performance suggest its premium valuation might be increasingly justified in the current volatile market environment as investors come around to the defensive qualities of this strong, globally diversified business. I will be eagerly awaiting earnings to either confirm or reassess my near-term outlook on the stock, but my long-term outlook has remained bullish nonetheless.

data.title: Federal Reserve Watch: A 'New' Round
data.description:
Last month, the Federal Reserve announced a "new round" of quantitative tightening.

The current rounds of quantitative tightening began in the middle of March 2022.

The Federal Reserve began to reduce the size of its securities portfolio.

Here is the chart showing the "first round" going into the middle of September 2024.

The second round of "quantitative tightening" went from September 18, 2024 up until this past Wednesday, April 2. 2025.

The third round of "quantitative tightening," according to Federal Reserve announcements, is supposed to begin at the start of April.

And, here it is at the start of April, so the assumption is that the Federal Reserve is now in its third round of "quantitative tightening."

The Federal Reserve is still aiming to continually to reduce the size of its securities portfolio, but at a slower rate than it had been reducing it in the second round.

During the second round of quantitative tightening, the Federal Reserve reduced the size of its securities portfolio by $275.7 billion.

Furthermore, during this period of time, the Federal Reserve saw its "loans" to commercial banks decline by $94.7 billion, further reducing the dependence of the commercial banking system on the Federal Reserve.

The Federal Reserve has used these loan facilities to support the commercial banking system, as it helped the banking system adjust its reserve position throughout this period of monetary adjustment.

Note: there are only $4.0 billion of these loans still on the Fed's balance sheet.

So this second round of quantitative easing continued the Fed's efforts to reduce the amount of "cash" that the Fed had injected into the banking system to get the financial system through the disturbances of the early 2020s.

Note that the Federal Reserve System still has a portfolio of securities held outright of $6.4 trillion

How things have changed.

In the middle of March 2022, before the first round of quantitative tightening, the Federal Reserve held $8.5 trillion of securities held outright

Before the first round of 2020 year of quantitative easing, the Federal Reserve had $3.8 trillion of securities held outright.

And, during the Great Recession in February 2009, the Federal Reserve had $512.0 billion in securities held outright.

Can you believe that a little more than fifteen years ago, the Federal Reserve had a portfolio that was about $6.0 trillion smaller in terms of securities held outright!

My, things have changed!

The "excess reserves" in the banking system have also experienced a dramatic change during this time.

On January 9, 2008, there were $4.1 billion in "excess reserves" in the commercial banking system.

On April 2, 2025, the banking system held $3.4 trillion in "excess reserves"!

The banking system in the United States has lots and lots and lots of "excess reserves" around to lend out...to generate inflation.

And, this is where the banking system resides right now.

The Federal Reserve believes there is still too much "cash" on the balance sheets of the commercial banking system.

Federal Reserve officials still believe that the Fed needs a monetary policy that is defined as "quantitative tightening."

However, the Federal Reserve believes that it still must accommodate the political pressures that are alive and well in the world, but must not put too much further stress upon the banks by trying to reduce its securities portfolio too quickly going forward.

What about the Fed's policy rate of interest...the Federal Funds rate?

The Federal Reserve has been reducing its policy rate of interest as Fed officials feels comfortable reducing the rate.

Right now, Federal Reserve officials, to me, seem very cautious about taking the policy rate down further too fast.

Federal Reserve officials have done a very good job in getting the rate down to where it is now, but there is so much uncertainty being generated by the Trump administration with its tariffs and so forth, that these officials don't want to get into a situation where they move their policy rate down, but then must immediately return it to its previous level.

Federal Reserve officials don't want to get into a position where they are constantly forced to move the policy rate one way and then reverse themselves in the near future.

"Stability" is much sought after.

Chairman Jerome Powell has always been one to, if anything, err on the side of stability. This time around is no different.

So, the Trump tariffs are hitting the world and are being responded to.

World stock markets are reacting to all that is going on and will continue to do so.

And, uncertainty will seriously cloud the future.

Let us hope that Mr. Powell and the Federal Reserve are able to provide some source of stability as the United States goes forward.

data.title: Celestica: Valuation Is Too Attractive To Ignore
data.description:
Introduction

Despite Celestica's (CLS) stock is down 32% since my January 'Strong Buy' recommendation, I do not panic because this nosedive is explained by the overall stock market panic triggered by President Trump's tariffs. Meanwhile, Celestica's fundamentals demonstrate improvements. The latest quarterly performance was strong and there are several other positive developments as well. The valuation is very attractive after the sharp dip, and I am inclined to confirm my 'Strong Buy' rating.

Fundamental analysis

The company still enjoys strong AI tailwinds. Despite some slight deceleration due to increasing comparatives, the quarterly year-over-year revenue growth is still around 20%. Quarterly estimates from Wall Street analysts, suggest that Celestica will maintain double-digit revenue CAGR for at least the next eight quarters.

It is very important that it is not just revenue growth at all costs, but high-quality revenue growth. The company's TTM EPS is at record levels after more than tripling since the beginning of FY2023. And such an aggressive EPS growth is projected to continue, according to Wall Street analysts. Celestica's EPS is expected to more than double by FY2028.

Thus, the company is very efficient in creating value for its shareholders during the current AI and data center boom. Therefore, technology hyperscalers' plans to invest more than $300 billion in data centers during 2025 is a big industry tailwind for Celestica. To improve its positioning in the AI revolution, Celestica significantly ramps up innovation spending. The company's R&D budget has more than doubled since FY2022.

Such a firm commitment to innovation is not only crucial in driving future growth but is also important in building the company's image as an innovative employer. Everything looks good from this perspective as the company was recognized as one of Canada's top employers for young people for the second consecutive year. This is a crucial positive factor over the long haul as being one of the country's top employers means that Celestica is more likely to hire the brightest young employees with great potential to develop.

Celestica's robust balance sheet also positions the company well to invest in growth and innovation. The company is in a strong liquidity position with more than $400 million in cash. Celestica's total debt of around $930 million is mostly long-term, and it is around 10 times lower than the company's market cap. The company's tangible book value per share has demonstrated consistent growth over the last three years. In addition, S&P Global recently upgraded Celestica's credit rating to 'BB+'.

The company's fundamental strength attracts new investors, including institutional ones. For example, LPL Financial LLC boosted its stake in Celestica significantly over the last quarter. Overall, the number of Celestica's institutional investors has increased significantly over the last few years. Increasing confidence in Celestica's long-term prospects from institutional investors is a solid bullish factor, in my opinion.

Wall Street's analysts are also confident in Celestica's ability to successfully capitalize on AI tailwinds. The average target price is $140, which is more than twice as high as the last close. In addition, the stock also boasts a 'Strong Buy' rating from Seeking Alpha Quant, which is usually quite strong in stock picking.

Furthermore, the technical setup looks excellent. Celestica's 14-day RSI indicator is very low at just 31, meaning that the stock is oversold. Celestica's RSI indicator rarely dips that low, indicating potential for a solid near-term share price rebound.

Wrapping this up, Celestica's fundamentals position the company well to continue boosting value for shareholders by capitalizing on AI tailwinds. Increasing confidence from institutional investors, as well as massive upside potential, suggested from Wall Street analysts add significant optimism as well.

Valuation analysis

Celestica's forward P/E ratio trajectory looks extremely bullish. The estimated FY2025 P/E ratio is already quite low, below 15. Furthermore, due to the expected aggressive EPS growth, the FY2028 forward P/E ratio is expected to be as low as 7.4.

Now, the DCF it is. As there were no material changes in interest rates, I reiterate the same 8.3% discount rate. AI tailwinds remain quite strong, meaning that using a 3% constant growth rate to calculate terminal value ('TV') is quite conservative. Top-line assumptions for FY 2026-2027 were obtained from consensus because they represent the average opinions of at least 5 Wall Street analysts. For the years beyond FY2027, I incorporate a conservative 100 basis points yearly revenue growth deceleration. Celestica's TTM levered FCF margin is 3.25%. I am using it as FY2026 assumption and also applying a 50-basis point yearly expansion rate for the FCF margin. According to Seeking Alpha, there are 116 million shares outstanding.

The suggested fair share price is $116.5. This is significantly higher than the current share price with a 68% upside potential. I find Celestica's valuation extremely attractive.

Mitigating factors

It is highly uncertain regarding how long the broader stock market panic will take. Trump's administration has been quite aggressive with new tariffs so far, and it is possible that new big developments will emerge in this respect. Even the stock market's largest names like Apple and Nvidia experienced a massive stock sell-off this week. For smaller players like Celestica, share price movements are highly likely to be sharper. Therefore, I do not recommend buying CLS without a long-term mindset. I do not suggest to time the market.

It is also important to keep in mind that Celestica faces a massive customer concentration risk. According to the 10-K, Celestica's top 10 customers represented 73% of total revenue in FY2024. It is also important to understand that with massive revenue growth from data centers, the concentration risk increases because in FY2023 Celestica's top 10 customers generated 64% of the company's sales. High customer concentration is inherently risky, as it makes any company heavily dependent on its partners' decisions and financial strength.

Conclusion

My take is that Celestica remains a high-conviction 'Strong buy'. The company's fundamentals are consistently improving, which is recognized by institutional investors, credit agencies, and various reputable analysts.

data.title: New U.S. Tariffs Forcing Shippers To Recalculate Supply Chains On The Fly
data.description:
The broad and varied tariffs announced this week by the US administration that affect US imports from virtually every country around the globe have sent shippers scrambling, forcing a rethink of supply chain strategy within a market so cloaked in uncertainty that one stakeholder likened it to the early days of the COVID-19 pandemic.

Some US-based shippers are, incredibly enough, racing to beat the April 9 imposition of new tariffs on most goods, while others are holding back products in Asia on the chance the levies will be reduced by US President Donald Trump, whose tariff implementation strategy in recent weeks has been marked by a large dose of impulsiveness.

April 9 is the date country-specific tariffs take effect. Baseline 10% tariffs on countries that avoided the specific, so-called "reciprocal" rates are due to go into effect April 5.

"It looks to me like we are witnessing a sudden global slowdown akin to COVID," a forwarder who did not want to be identified told the Journal of Commerce. "It’s starting to look like the collective response will be to stop, kind of like what we saw when COVID started. The question is, for how long?"

The source said some major customers are “deciding to store product in Asia and essentially cutting off the flow.”

“They are looking at using air or other modes but in more of a ‘batch’ sequencing versus the normal flow models,” the forwarder said.

Another forwarder who also did not want to be identified said shippers are using a range of tools ahead of the tariff increases.

“Customers are shifting volume from [full containerload] to [less-than-containerload] to get goods here before April 9,” the source said. “Some are booking air to get ahead of the tariffs. Some are pulling back orders. Some vendors are pulling back on DDP [delivery duty paid] shipments to renegotiate on price. Some want to change the Incoterms to change the responsible party for duties.”

DDP occurs when the seller is responsible for paying duties.

The forwarder added that some customers that were eyeing an exit from China to other sourcing locations in Asia are now rethinking those plans. That’s because those alternative sourcing locations have been nailed with new tariffs, too, most notably Vietnam at 46%.

Robert Khachatryan, CEO of Los Angeles-based forwarder FreightRight, said he isn’t seeing a rush to expedite bookings that would not have ordinarily moved this week.

“Clients that have bookings in place are pushing to make sure they depart as planned,” Khachatryan said. “However, there are no new orders rushing in.”

‘Structural change’ to supply chains

The Trump tariff plan, which was far more extensive than observers expected, is forcing a wholesale reconsideration of individual supply chains.

“With the new tariffs and the end of the de minimis exemption, many importers are being forced to rethink how they manage cost, visibility, and compliance across their supply chains,” said Jim McCullen, chief technology officer at Century Supply Chain Solutions. “What we’re seeing is that this moment isn’t just about logistics, it’s about risk management, planning, and structural change.”

In some ways, the race for vessel capacity has been on since before Trump took office for the second time. Aggregate import volumes into the ports of Los Angeles and Long Beach in January and February exceeded even the peak COVID-19 years of 2021 and 2022, and were even 24% higher than pre-pandemic January–February volumes in 2019 when frontloading occurred to beat Trump’s initial tranche of 25% tariffs on Chinese imports.

The new tariffs are designed to reinvigorate the US manufacturing sector, Trump said during a White House event Wednesday. But they come amid a slowing manufacturing sector in North America that showed weakness in part because of the long-threatened tariffs.

US factory output fell in March, according to Purchasing Managers’ Index survey data compiled by S&P Global, parent company of the Journal of Commerce. Respondents attributed the slowdown to falling demand and the waning impact from the cargo frontloading that occurred earlier in the year as importers moved to beat the imposition of tariffs

“Manufacturing output fell across North America in March at one of the steepest rates seen over the past two years ... with broad-based production loses in the US, Canada and Mexico,” Chris Williamson, chief business economist at S&P Global Market Intelligence, wrote in a research note Thursday. “Prices paid for inputs also rose sharply. US factory input cost inflation, in fact, exceeded that seen in all 33 economies surveyed by S&P Global for the first time since 2008.”

Consumer goods in crosshairs

Early research into which commodity categories might be most impacted by the tariffs highlights the effect on finished consumer goods from high-tariff nations.

“Notable examples include toys and video games [average duty rate increase of 30.4%], computer parts [29.6%], clothing [28.3%, with some as high as 36.9%] and smartphones [27%],” Chris Rogers, head of supply chain research at S&P Global Market Intelligence, wrote in an analysis Thursday. “Many of the products that are excluded from these new duties will face their own tariff reviews and may end up with duties of around 25%, including copper, lumber, pharmaceuticals and semiconductors, after the reviews are completed in a few months.”

The hit would be $458 billion to US importers’ costs in a full year, based on 2024 import levels, Rogers added.

“The full impact on supply chains will also be a function of how other governments react — for example, the EU and mainland China have been more hawkish — and how firms choose to incorporate the new tariffs into their pricing and sourcing strategies,” he wrote. “Given the wide-ranging nature of the duties and focus on consumer goods, price increases may be a main strategy followed.”

UBS, meanwhile, said it expects the tariffs announced Wednesday, in addition to previously announced 25% tariffs on all imported automobiles, to drive pressure on international trade volumes and weaken domestic freight activity. In a research note Thursday, the bank said it estimates that 33% of rail and 25% of truck ton miles in the US are directly tied to international trade.

Whether US Customs and Border Protection (CBP) has the capacity and sufficient technology infrastructure in place to enforce the new tariffs is another open question. Some forwarders said the agency can handle the changes, while others were doubtful given disruptions caused by previous tariff implementations.

“Customs is almost guaranteed not to be ready,” Khachatryan said. “Even with the previous, less sweeping tariffs, CBP was woefully unprepared. Aluminum tariffs alone took over a week to work properly in different [automated brokerage interface] systems, with many shipments stuck in storage at airline terminals. Guidance for customs brokers is even slower to come. Something of this magnitude will almost certainly be a lot more disruptive, not least because of all the overlapping tariffs and confusing exclusions.”

More broadly, the tariffs could well lead to choppier decision-making for logistics operations personnel, with decisions on whether to import goods or hold them back determined by Trump’s seemingly day-to-day policy changes.

“This will be a good example of honing skills to strategically manage based on daily information versus quarterly cycles, and I doubt many will be able to do this,” a forwarder said

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: BlackBerry's QNX Shines, But Stock Still Too Pricey Despite 50% Fall
data.description:
Introduction

If you haven't been following it lately, BlackBerry (NYSE:BB) (TSX:BB:CA) has transitioned far from its storied smartphone days and is now focused almost exclusively on software: secure communications for enterprise and government customers and embedded automotive software through its flagship QNX platform. The stock climbed to $6 a share by mid-February, but since then, it almost halved to $3.14 post-Liberation Day.

Today, I want to walk you through the story-where the company stands, recent earnings commentary, the industry outlook, what BB is getting right, my concerns and the valuation-so that you have a clearer picture of this once-iconic name.

Company Overview

BlackBerry's legacy as a smartphone pioneer is long behind it. These days, the firm splits its attention between:

Secure Communications (previously the core of its "cybersecurity" unit), which caters to highly regulated industries such as government and finance. This segment represents 51% of total revenues and had an EBITDA margin of 19% in FY25.

QNX Automotive Software (within the "Internet of Things" or IoT segment), an operating system found in millions of vehicles worldwide to power everything from infotainment to safety-critical systems. It represents 44% of revenues and has a higher EBITDA margin than Secure Communications at 25%.

Licensing. This segment represents 5% of revenues and has an EBITDA margin of 60%. This segment monetizes the large patent portfolio.

Recent years have been marked by divestments and reorganizations. Most prominently, BlackBerry sold its Cylance endpoint-security business in late 2024. BB sold Cylance to Arctic Wolf for $160 million plus a small amount of stock, far lower than the $1.4 billion BB paid for the business back in 2018. This move improved the company's bottom-line profile by removing a major money-loser and allowed management to focus on areas where BB has clearer strengths-particularly QNX. Despite these efforts, the secure communications side faces stiff competition, and short-term headwinds (like potential US government contract cuts) are affecting growth expectations.

Recent Earnings Call Highlights

On April 2, BB reported fourth-quarter earnings results. While BB beat consensus estimates, the share price dropped due to the weak 2026 guidance.

BB beat top-line and EPS expectations, showing that near-term execution is improving. Revenues were $141.7 million, a decrease of -18.1% y/y but beat expectations by $8.9 million. EPS for the quarter was $0.03 while the consensus estimate was zero.

However, management issued weak guidance for fiscal 2026, attributing this partly to a cautious outlook on US federal contract renewals and geopolitical tensions-issues weighing on its secure communications business. The guidance implies flat revenues in the best-case scenario, with the lower end of the guidance implying a 6% decrease. The guidance expects revenue growth in QNX offset by a shrinkage in revenues from Secure Communications. The midpoint EBITDA is slightly below the consensus.

The IoT (QNX) segment is holding up better, but management is worried about automotive tariffs and a conservative spending environment among automakers. However, during the earnings call, management mentioned that 50% of QNX revenues come from outside the US, providing some insulation.

Industry Outlook

BB sits at the crossroads of two very different software markets: Automotive/IoT Software and Enterprise & Government Secure Communications.

The vehicle software space is rapidly expanding. Cars are growing more software-defined and connected every year. BB's QNX is considered a top-tier embedded OS for safety-critical applications; major automakers worldwide deploy it for infotainment and digital cockpit features. Over time, higher vehicle autonomy and connectivity levels could multiply BB's "software content per vehicle," fueling faster growth.

The secure communications segment is more crowded, with fierce competition from giant cybersecurity vendors (like Microsoft (MSFT) or CrowdStrike (CRWD)) and specialized software companies. BB retains a loyal niche in regulated industries, but overall growth rates have disappointed, and management has repeatedly scaled back expectations. Federal budget uncertainties, plus cyclical swings in government spending, can quickly affect short-term revenue.

What BlackBerry Gets Right: Automotive and Cloud Partnerships

I think BB is heading in the right direction. By shedding poor performers (like Cylance) and redirecting resources to IoT, management is gravitating to the segment where it has a true competitive edge. That should enhance margins and long-term growth.

QNX is a recognized leader in safety and security in embedded automotive software. With cars getting "smarter," BB stands to capture more revenue per vehicle over time.

Also, I like the collaborations with AWS on the IVY platform-and recently with other major cloud players. This could entrench BB further in next-generation connected cars.

My four concerns with BlackBerry

Concern #1: Weak Cybersecurity Position

While still retaining a strong brand in regulated industries, BB's secure communications unit must fight behemoths like Microsoft, Cisco (CSCO), and others with bigger R&D budgets. Cybersecurity demands constant R&D investment to remain competitive. While the R&D expenditure below is not purely on cybersecurity, it is an illustration of the considerable budgets competitors are playing with.

Concern #2: Reliance on US Government Spending

Over one-fifth of BB's secure communications revenue depends on US federal contracts, as mentioned by John Giamatteo during the earnings call:

U.S. federal is a substantial portion. I would say probably, 20%, 25% of our overall Secure Comms business comes with the U.S. federal business...

Any sustained cuts or delays can significantly dent top-line results. I believe anything related to the federal government may be impacted due to DOGE.

Concern #3: Uncertain Path to Consistent Profitability

Despite software margins, BB's full pivot to software has been rocky. It has posted inconsistent revenue trends for years.

Concern #4: Intense Automotive Competition

While QNX is recognized for safety, free operating systems like Linux or Android threaten to displace it in certain corners. To keep its edge, BB must continually invest, thus pressuring margin expansion.

Moreover, automotive tariffs are a headwind for QNX. Automotive tariffs to Canada were maintained during the Liberation Day announcement. Also, the reciprocal tariffs announced will have an impact on global supply chains that will spill over to BB.

Valuation

On April 2, BB's stock dropped... twice. First, it dropped from $3.73 to $3.38 after the earnings release, and then it dropped further to $3.14 with the Liberation Day announcement.

While on a P/E and EV/EBITDA basis, the shares are trading at the lowest level in the past 52 weeks. It is still a rich valuation of 32x forward earnings and 24x forward EBITDA.

Even on a relative basis, the shares are overpriced for the sector.

That premium would be justified for a growing company and/or with a competitive advantage. However, BB has none of that, so a premium wouldn't be justified.

Conclusion

BB's transformation from phones to software is an ongoing saga-one with some bright spots, notably its QNX platform in the IoT space, and one with many uncertainties, especially in its secure communications business. While the shares have dropped from over $6 to $3, the shares remain expensive on a relative and absolute basis, so there isn't a screaming discount here.

data.title: Surging Trade Deficit Likely To Reverse In The Months Ahead
data.description:
The Trade Deficit is one of the two components of the twin deficits; the other being the federal budget deficit, which was last reviewed in January. The trade deficit used to be a number that received a ton of attention in the 1980s and 1990s because it was determined to be a strong gauge of the strength and weakness in the US economy. It was last positive in 1975, but big moves in the trade deficit through the '80s and '90s could impact the stock market.

Lately, not many people focus on the trade deficit numbers; however, it is still an excellent metric for identifying how the US is performing. How much more are we consuming than we are producing? It also allows us to export our inflation abroad. If the rest of the world decides to stop making things for us, then it could be an ugly transition.

Current Trends

The February trade deficit came in at -$123B, which was the second-largest on record only behind the January trade deficit of -$131B. This surge is driven by people trying to get products into the US before tariffs are enacted.

Once the tariffs go into effect, we are likely to see a big contraction in the trade deficit. Not only have we made advance purchases on goods with the recent surge, but the tariffs will make foreign goods more expensive, denting demand.

Figure: 1 Monthly Plot Detail

The table below provides detail and shows the massive surge in imports YoY. The TTM total deficit has surged 25%!

Figure: 2 Trade Balance Detail

Historical Perspective

Zooming out and focusing on the net numbers shows the longer-term trend. It really puts the last two months into perspective. It is unlikely this continues, and we will probably see a reversal back to the old trend line or even above it as tariffs put a dent in trade.

Figure: 3 Historical Net Trade Balance

The Services Surplus has been drifting lower ever since the election as well.

Figure: 4 Historical Services Surplus

To put it all together and remove some of the noise, the next plot below shows the Trailing Twelve-Month (TTM) values for each month (i.e., each period represents the summation of the previous 12 months). The dip can be seen from the last two months on the far right.

Figure: 5 Trailing 12 Months (TTM)

The TTM Net Trade Deficit surge has brought the deficit as a % of GDP to 3.48%. This is still well below the Covid years and pre-financial crisis.

Figure: 6 TTM vs GDP

The chart below shows the YTD values. While Exports are mostly flat, the Imports have seen a big surge.

Figure: 7 Year to Date

Wrapping Up

The Trade Deficit still gets very little attention, but it serves as a huge windfall and potential risk for the US. Currently, foreign countries are willing to provide goods and services in exchange for USD that far exceeds what they are buying with the USD they earn.

Trump is playing with fire by targeting our trade partners. If we see Imports start to slow dramatically in the months ahead, it could result in a big spike in prices. The huge deficit surges these last two months are anomalies

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Hydro One: A Strong Q4, But Still Too Expensive
data.description:
Introduction

Hydro One (TSX:H:CA)(OTCPK:HRNNF) stands out as a high-quality, recession resilient business that, I think, would make sense in a lot of portfolios. In August, I highlighted why Ontario's regulated utility company offered a strong moat and predictable cash flow. In its latest quarterly results, Q4'24 exceeded expectations, with solid growth in both revenue and net income. The company benefited from higher distribution rates approved by the Ontario Energy Board, along with successful cost-saving measures that improved efficiency. The completion of major transmission projects ahead of schedule and under budget were among the positives, but I remain cautious on valuation. With Hydro One trading at a significant premium compared to both its peers and historical averages, I feel the stock may be priced for perfection which limits future upside.

Recent Results

Hydro One reported Q4’24 results that came in ahead of consensus estimates on both the top and bottom line. For the fourth quarter, the company reported revenues of $2.10 billion which was 5.9% higher compared to last year and $34.8 million ahead of analyst expectations. Revenues net of purchased power climbed 4.7% in Q4 with distribution revenues surging 11.5% thanks to lower earnings sharing, and higher rates approved by the Ontario Energy Board though transmission revenues dipped slightly by 0.2% due to lower peak demand. On the bottom line, net income climbed 6.5% compared to last year with EPS coming in at $0.33, 3 cents higher than last year and a beat of 2 cents relative to estimates.

When we look at what led to the results this quarter, Hydro One benefitted from a steady operating environment with margins holding firm as maintenance and administration costs dropped 6% compared to last year with transmission down 9.2% and distribution down 11.3% driven by reduced work program spending. I think this cost discipline underscores the fact that Hydro One has been making good progress on realizing cost savings which paired with $150 million in productivity savings signals efficiency gains that I think the market responded positively too. Since the announcement of the results, shares of Hydro One are making 52-week highs while the result of the market tumbles amidst tariff worries.

In terms of some of the key highlights from the quarter, one of the big developments I think was noteworthy was the completion of Chatham by Lakeshore Transmission project a year ahead of schedule and $30 million under budget, marking a milestone as Hydro One’s first 50/50 First Nations equity partnership. Elsewhere, construction kicked off on the Waasigan Transmission Line in Northwest Ontario with a 2027 target while Hydro One landed the Wawa and Porcupine transmission line project a 260-kilometer line slated for 2030. The $257 million acquisition of a 48% stake in the East West Tie line bolsters its transmission network and put together, reinforces the company’s focus on being a premier electrification supplier.

Given the developments around building out a new transmission project at Chatham and Waasigan, Hydro One’s capital allocation leaned heavy on investment this year with $3.1 billion in capex for 2024, up 21% from 2023. With no NCIB in place or any share repurchase activity, the company’s remaining cash flow goes towards paying its dividend with the board declaring a $0.3142 quarterly dividend. Since its inception as a public company, Hydro One has maintained a growing dividend for 8 years now and with a payout ratio of 45%, the company’s dividend looks safe.

The dividend safety is further backed up by a strong balance sheet, something I think is critical to look at when analyzing utilities. In the case of Hydro One, while the company did issue $2.75 billion in medium-term notes issued in 2024, we can see that the total debt has only increased marginally over the last several quarters. Moreover, in terms of ratios, there’s been little change as Annualized FFO to Net Debt of 13.4% was down just slightly from last year’s 13.8% and the company’s change in net debt to capitalization ratio of 58.4% compared to last year’s 57.2% was de minimis. As well, with long-term debt ratings of A from S&P and A3 from Moody’s,

Outlook

In terms of the outlook, I think there’s good reason to be optimistic about the company’s financial performance going forward. With 2027 guidance for between $2.15 to $2.37 in EPS (up from $2.05 to $2.26), management is forecasting a 7% annualized growth rate at the midpoint based off of a 2022 normalized base of $1.61. To me, this bump reflects confidence in near-term drivers like the East West Tie acquisition and $300 million in rate base growth from rural broadband alongside stronger-than-expected segment performance so this guidance sets a clear bar for the next few years signaling the company’s intent to capitalize on Ontario’s electrification wave without overpromising.

Unlike other utilities that have faced some pressure on rate base growth, in the short-term, I think the coming quarters should see continued benefits from Q4’s momentum with lower OM&A costs and higher OEB-approved rates. Longer term, I’m optimistic about Hydro One’s positioning as Ontario’s transmission leader, especially with nine major transmission lines slated through 2032. In my view, the company’s $3.1 billion 2024 capex and plans for sustained investment point to a rate base that could accelerate beyond the current 6% CAGR guidance post-2027. The broadband initiative, while now at the low end of $300 million to $700 million, could surprise to the upside if ISP applications pick up steam and I think this has a good chance of driving some moderate earnings growth.

Along with Chatham by Lakeshore partnership and the Wawa-Porcupine line, these initiative should support long-term steady growth off of these infrastructure investments in Ontario. That said, I’m mindful of headwinds like potential tariffs or inflation that could nudge costs up though the $150 million in productivity savings this year gives me confidence in their ability to offset pressures. I’ll discuss more about this in the ‘Risks’ section.

When we look out beyond 2027 guidance, I see Hydro One navigating the next regulatory reset in 2028 with tailwinds rather than headwinds, which could be a shift from past cycles. The joint rate application filing in fall 2026 will be a key moment and while no firm guidance will come until approval, I expect the company to push for rates that reflect its outsized growth compared to peers. The interplay of population growth, industrial demand, and transmission expansion could drive earnings well into the 2030s, especially if nuclear projects like Bruce Power’s expansion materialize needing new lines by 2035-2040. I feel the lack of asset sales or heavy debt reliance in the next period keeps the balance sheet flexible for these opportunities.

Risks

In terms of some of the areas I think investors should watch for risks, regulatory uncertainty stands out as my biggest worry. While the join rate application fall 2026 (and its expected approval by 2028) will reset productivity gains and earnings sharing which could throttle EPS growth below the 6% to 8% range management’s projecting, there could be a scenario where the Ontario Energy Board bows to political pressure to keep rates low (eg. tightening return on equity or equity ratios) and that might choke the earnings trajectory here on those future years. I feel this risk looms larger in Ontario’s charged political environment, where energy affordability often trumps utility growth ambitions. This could flip the script from a supportive regulatory tailwind to a growth-stifling headwind if the next rate period doesn’t match Hydro One’s expansion plans, so it’s something investors need to watch out for.

The second-biggest risk I see would be on interest rates. While it’s my view that it’s a fool’s game to try to predict what will happen with rates, it’s clear that higher interest rates are a net negative for utility companies and we’re likely to be in a higher for longer rate environment. Rates are down 225bps from their peak at 5% and while it’s possible that we could still get another 50-75bps in the medium term, I don’t see rates going down as much as people think.

On the topic of rates, this has a direct implication for a company like Hydro One as the company’s most recent quarter saw a 7.5% jump in interest expense compared to last year as a result of more expensive debt. There’s also more debt on the books compared to last year with debt growing by $2.75 billion in medium-term notes issued in 2024. Minimizing interest rate risk is the fact that Hydro One has long-term debt ratings of A from S&P and A3 from Moody’s, so the company can access capital markets at extremely low rates.

Valuation and Wrap Up

While I’ve warmed up on Hydro One’s financials and business outlook, I’m still of the view that shares of the company are still way too expensive. When we look at analyst price targets, most have shifted negative, with 2 new ‘sell’ ratings in the past year. With 10 ‘hold’ ratings and 3 ‘sell’ ratings, the analysts have an average price target of $45.19 which implies about 10.3% downside form current levels.

This makes sense when we look at Hydro One’s multiples. At the current valuation of 24.3x P/E and 14.3x EV/EBITDA, the company trades well above peers at 17.7x P/E and 11.8x EV/EBITDA (source: Bloomberg). At a 37% premium to the company’s five year average P/E and a 22% premium to the company’s average EV/EBITDA, I think forward returns are likely going to be lower than what investors have seen in the past.

With a 7% annualized growth rate in EPS going forward, higher than the company’s previous 5% growth in the decade between 2010-2020, I can understand how the company’s valuation has re-rated. That said, I think Hydro One trades expensively relative to peers and looks priced for perfection. With government yields around the 3% level, Canadian insurers yielding 4%, and Canadian banks paying dividends of 5%+, this isn’t exactly a cheap stock when we consider that it trades at the same valuation as some Mag7 stocks like Microsoft (MSFT) or Google (GOOG) which are set to grow at higher rates and have fortress balance sheets. For this reason, I’m not a buyer and maintain my ‘sell’ rating on Hydro One.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: VLU: A Smarter Value Approach To Long-Term Returns
data.description:
ETF Overview

SPDR S&P 1500 Value Tilt ETF (NYSEARCA:VLU) invests in a portfolio of over 1,400 U.S. stocks with relatively lower valuations. The fund’s expense ratio of 0.12% is not expensive relative to other value funds. For example, iShares S&P 500 Value ETF (IVE) has a higher expense ratio of 0.18%. VLU offers a unique value strategy by tilting toward lower valuation stocks. The fund is not market-cap weighted but weighted by each stocks' valuation score and include more traditional growth stocks than other pure value funds. The result is a portfolio of larger-cap stocks and with higher exposure to technology sector. This resulted in its better performance in the past and lower downside risk. Therefore, the fund appears to be a good choice for value-oriented investors.

Fund Analysis

VLU’s portfolio construction methodology

Let’s begin by reviewing how VLU constructs its portfolio. The fund tracks the S&P 1500 Low Valuation Tilt Index, which gives greater weight to stocks with relatively low valuations and less weight to those with higher valuations. In other words, VLU is not a market-cap weighted fund. Instead, the fund is weighted by their valuation. This valuation is based on 5 important valuation metrics: price to book ratio, price to earnings ratio, price to cash flow ratio, price to sales ratio, and dividends paid.

The fund may include more technology stocks than other pure value funds

Unlike traditional value funds, this fund may include stocks with lower valuation stocks but are categorized as growth stocks. For example, Amazon (AMZN), the third-largest holding and account for about 2.0% of VLU’s portfolio, is more of a growth stock than a value stock. However, it received higher weight because in certain valuation metrics such as price to sales and price to cash flow ratios, it scored better than other growth stocks.

In fact, VLU has a higher exposure to technology than other traditional pure value funds. Below is a table that compares VLU’s sector composition with Invesco S&P 1500 Pure Value ETF (RPV), a pure value fund that selects only stocks with strong value characteristics, and is market-cap weighted. As can be seen from the table below, technology stocks account for about 11.7% of VLU’s portfolio, whereas it only comprises about 2.2% of RPV’s portfolio. Therefore, VLU has higher exposure to technology than RPV.

Sectors (%)

VLU

RPV

Basic Materials

2.33

5.59

Consumer Cyclical

9.35

13.77

Financial Services

22.24

16.80

Real Estate

3.26

1.12

Communication Services

9.99

5.72

Energy

8.52

9.65

Industrials

8.52

4.11

Technology

11.72

2.18

Consumer Defensive

8.49

16.02

Healthcare

11.10

21.05

Utilities

4.48

3.99

Source: Morningstar (accessed on April 3, 2025), created by author

As we know, technology sector has been one of the best growth sectors among all other sectors in the past and is poised to deliver strong growth in the upcoming decades thanks to many technological megatrends such as artificial intelligence, AR/VR, cloud computing, etc. Therefore, VLU should have better growth profile than RPV.

VLU is underweight in defensive sectors

On the other hand, VLU’s exposure to defensive sectors is limited relative to RPV. As the table above also shows, VLU’s exposure to defensive sectors such as consumer defensive, healthcare, and utilities is about 24.1%. In contrast, these sectors represent about 41.1% of RPV’s portfolio. Since defensive sectors tend to have lower growth potential than most other sectors, it is clear that VLU should have better performance in the long run.

VLU has outperformed S&P 1500 Pure Value

VLU’s underweight in defensive and overweight in technology has indeed resulted in its stronger performance in the past. As can be seen from the chart below, VLU delivered a total return of 187.1% in the past 10 years, much better than RPV’s 115.9%.

VLU is tilted towards large-cap

Now, we will examine VLU’s fund style. As can be seen from the table below, about 59% of VLU’s portfolio belong to large-cap stocks. Mid-cap and small-cap stocks only represent 28% and 12% of its portfolio respectively. This composition is quite different from RPV. As the table below also shows, large-cap stocks account for only 15% of RPV’s portfolio. On the other hand, mid-cap stocks account for about two-thirds of RPV’s portfolio. To summarize, VLU has higher exposure to larger-cap stocks while RPV is tilted towards smaller-cap stocks.

VLU

RPV

Large-Cap

59%

15%

Mid-Cap

28%

66%

Small-Cap

12%

19%

Source: Morningstar (accessed on April 4, 2025), created by author

VLU has lower downside risk

As the chart below shows, VLU has lower downside risk than RPV in almost every correction in the past 10 years. VLU’s overweight in larger-cap stocks may be a key reason to its lower downside risk. As we know, larger-cap stocks tend to have stronger balance sheets and well-established than smaller-cap stocks. They are like larger boats that should have much smoother ride in any storms than smaller boats. Therefore, it is not a surprise to see VLU fare better in market turmoil.

Investor Takeaway

VLU’s value strategy has created a portfolio with higher exposure to large-cap stocks and technology stocks than traditional pure value funds. The fund has delivered stronger returns and have lower downside risk. Therefore, this fund appears to be a compelling choice for value-oriented investors.

Additional Disclosure: This is not financial advice and that all financial investments carry risks. Investors are expected to seek financial advice from professionals before making any investment.

data.title: Apple's Downward Spiral: A Crisis It Can't Escape
data.description:
Apple Inc. (NASDAQ:AAPL) is a $3.4 trillion luxury gadget company, with one of the most well-known and desirable brands in the tech space and a phenomenal ecosystem of products. With its innovation-first approach, many consider Apple to be a leader of the consumer-facing technological domain, as a result of its phenomenal marketing of cutting edge products.

While Apple Inc. has seen phenomenal growth in recent years, the current tariff situation threatens to disproportionately disrupt its competitive position and pricing power. President Trump's heavy tariffs against imports from Asia, and in particularly from China significantly hurt the company's prospects, and could result in a fall in demand for its products. This issue is exacerbated as Apple's core markets face off the risk of a recession, which could result in a slowdown in luxury spending.

Valuation metrics do not offer any justification for Apple's current prices, especially in light of the bleak position tariffs put it in. I rate AAPL as a sell.

A Recap of Events

On Wednesday, President Trump doubled down on his tariff policy, by unveiling a sweeping list of "reciprocal tariffs" laid out against 180 countries. The sharpest of these were, as expected, against China, adding 34% in tariffs, against the 20% levy that had already been imposed. In fact, Asia collectively seems to be hit the hardest, with even Japan and Taiwan facing 24% and 32% tariff rates, respectively.

Immediately after the announcement, S&P 500 futures slid sharply by over 3.2%, while comparative benchmark indexes in Japan and Germany fell by 3% and 2%, respectively.

In the US stock market, all sectors seemed hit almost consistently hard, with the exception of the healthcare space:

As can be seen quite apparently, Apple Inc. has been hit the hardest amongst the most significant names in the stock market, sliding by almost 7.5% in premarket hour trading. Clearly the market is somewhat disproportionate in its feelings about the company being impacted by the tariff situation.

I concur with this view, and believe Apple stands to potentially see both the demand for its products and services fall, as a result of an increased cost burden to consumers, as well as higher retaliatory taxes in Europe. Both of these outcomes, collectively slash the company's potential to deliver value to its shareholders, and as such eliminate the justification for the premium seen in its price multiples.

Apple’s Overseas Manufacturing Presence Under Tariff Pressure

The immediate driver for Apple's larger-than-market selloff was its manufacturing presence spread across countries impacted by the tariffs, and in particular, China. In fact, most Apple products sold in the United States are manufactured in Chinese factories, while the US remains its largest market. This dynamic clearly compromises Apple's cost structure and competitive positioning.

In recent years, Apple has been actively working to insulate itself from deteriorating US-China trade relations, and has been expanding its manufacturing footprint in other regions. The most notable of these moves is India, where, according to Bernstein analysts, the company plans to have a fifth of all iPhones manufactured, by the end of the year. However, it is worth pointing out that the United States' sweeping tariff plan did not spare India, which faces a 27% levy, which somewhat neutralizes cost advantages Apple might have gained from its production shift. As a result, any return on the investments made from establishing a manufacturing presence outside China in India has sharply been diminished as a result.

However, compared to the 54% tariffs Chinese-manufactured products face, 27% levies against India, is a substantially better bullet to bite. However, Apple still is far from a position of ending its manufacturing reliance on China completely, and it is unlikely that the company can make a 100% shift away to a lower tariff region like India, in just a couple of years.

During the first Trump term, where trade tensions between the US and China were also heated, Apple CEO Tim Cook successfully persuaded President Trump to exempt Apple products shipped from China from tariffs, as Samsung products manufactured in South Korea were not subject to such trade barriers. As a result, the president gave Apple, an American company, a tariff waiver, to be better able to compete against a foreign rival. This time around, the situation is far more complex, and I believe that the possibility of such an exemption remains near zero. For one, among the primary reasons behind the President's bold tariff policy is to bring manufacturing in the United States. I do not see him making an exception for Apple, which produces excessively in China, a country that Donald Trump's conservative base strongly perceives as a fierce rival. Moreover, the argument that Samsung would have an advantage over Apple would not hold this time around, as South Korea also has been slapped with 26% tariffs.

Similarly, other regions such as Vietnam and Malaysia, where Apple has been building up a manufacturing presence also have been impacted, and charged with 46% and 24% tariffs, respectively.

As of yet, it is unclear how much of these cost increases will be absorbed by the company, affecting its profit margins, and how much will be passed over the consumers. While one case hurts Apple's financial position, the other hits at its marketing position. Both of these outcomes pose serious risk to the company's shareholders.

Recession and Retaliation Fears

Another, less immediate, angle to my bear case about Apple is the deteriorating macroeconomic environment that would result in falling demand for its products. Tariff uncertainty has already muted activity in the stock market, in recent months, causing the S&P 500 to underperform against benchmark indexes of other regions.

Moreover, analysts continue to increase the probability of a recession hitting the US economy within 2025. Most recently, Goldman Sachs, last week, increased their odds of a recession in the United States from 20% to about 35%, before the end of the year. The estimate was done before President Trump effectively announced tariffs against the world, and before impacted nations have drawn out retaliatory responses to said tariffs.

If a recession were to actualize in the United States, it would deal quite the blow to Apple, which has actively positioned itself as a premium, and high end tech brand, compared to competitors like Samsung. Recessions typically result in a boost in inflation and in unemployment, which causes broader markets to slow down their spending on luxury gadgets like iPhones. The bracket of individuals within the economy that can afford to buy a $1600 iPhone 16 Pro Max, or a $2300 iPad Pro sharply constricts in such dynamics, which would inevitably hurt Apple's sales.

This, of course, is the situation in the United States, Apple's largest geographic segment in terms of revenue. However, economists are warning that the disruption to the trade dynamics (as have been a norm for the most part of the past several decades), owing to tariff actions, could result in a global recession. In the UK, Barclays has predicted a 1.5% slowdown in overall GDP, as a result of the 10% baseline tariffs it has been subjected to. The European Union, another major market for Apple, has been hit with 20% tariffs, and Barclays expects its GDP to take a hit as substantial as 2.9%.

On the one hand, Apple is paying the price for having its manufacturing facilities based in China, and other regions, instead of America, on the other, it is caught in the middle of retaliatory actions targeting US-based tech giants. And to make matters worse, its financial and marketing positions stand to deteriorate in the macroeconomic turmoil that the current situation is leading up to. It's a lose-lose-lose situation for the company, from every angle.

Valuation Assessment

Seeking Alpha grades Apple on valuation at an F. On every multiple, it seems to be trading significantly higher than the median seen in the IT sector. For instance, its trailing PS ratio of 8.6 is about triple the tech sector median ratio of 2.8. Similarly, its trailing PCF ratio of 31.1 is significantly larger than the IT sector median PCF ratio of 17.6.

To me, this is quite the discrepancy because, as I have made the argument in prior sections, Apple seems to currently be facing one of the most challenging macroeconomic and business environment in the last decade, perhaps even more challenging than the slowdown seen during the pandemic scare during the Covid-19 era.

I believe that, given the immensity of current problems, where there is seemingly no way out for Apple from these challenges, older premiums on its multiples clearly no longer stand justified. In fact, given the increased risk of investing in the stock, at this stage in time, shareholders should be rewarded more, through cheaper price multiples, and not less, through inflated multiples, as we see.

I do not see its current pricing as being justified, and rate AAPL as a sell.

Risk to Thesis

The risk to any sell thesis, especially one as pessimistic as mine, is that any positives to the company, such as growth catalysts, are not being factored into the analysis. While I do consider Apple to be a luxury gadget company (and I'm sure some would disagree with me), there is no denying that the company has set a standard in the tech space, in terms of great brand value which commands undeniable brand loyalty and stellar tech innovation seen in its incredible ecosystem spanning the iPhone, iPad, Mac, and wearables.

Most recently, the company has been committed to its Apple Intelligence program, which has already been rolled out on most of its gadgets, giving the company exposure to strong AI tailwinds.

Before the AI proliferation boom, Apple was under a lot of pressure to continue rolling out groundbreaking innovations with each iteration of new models of its products. The iPhone, iPad, and Mac have all seen iterative improvements over the years, but none have fundamentally changed the user experience in a way that could truly be described as being radically transformative.

In my opinion, AI has opened the door for Apple to truly become one with the future, and enhance its entire ecosystem with artificial intelligence in ways that previously was not possible. Apple Intelligence stands to enhance user personalization, provide actually "intelligent" assistance from Siri, AI-driven photo and video editing, and transform overall user experience in a positive way.

Overall, I believe there is a good chance that Apple's products will continue to remain strongly desirable, and possibly even more so with latest AI-integrations which I see as some of its most transformative updates in the last decade. This desirability may very well give the company the edge it needs to survive in the uncertain, tariff-influenced environment that currently threatens it.

Investor Takeaway

I believe things look quite bleak for Apple, and the company hardly has any options to wiggle itself out of the current tariff enclosure it finds itself facing. On the one hand, the company is most likely going to end up having to drive up its prices due to tariffs, and also absorb a lot of that burden and see its profit margins chip away. On the other hand, it could be punished in Europe for being an American company, in retaliation to the tariffs imposed on the EU. All of this happens as the threat of a recession, in the US and in the global context, continue looking far more probable. In short, Apple is in a perfect storm right now. The best course of action for shareholders at this point seems to be to sell the stock and take their profits.
